1. Introduction
Promoting gender equality and empowering girls and women is a priority task of the Royal
Government of Cambodia in supporting line ministries and institutions to pay attention to promoting
gender equality. In this regard, the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sport has implemented the
Gender Mainstreaming Strategic Plan in Education 2021-2025 that achieves gender equality in the
education sector, in respond to the Roadmap for Sustainable Development Goals 2030 on Education
in Cambodia “Promoting Gender Equality and Empowering Women and Girls”, Target 1 “Reduce the
Gender Gap in Secondary Education”, the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of
Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW), the Royal Government's Pentagonal Strategy-Phase I, the
Neary Rattanak VI Strategic Plan, and the Education Strategic Plan 2024-2028. The Pentagonal
Strategy-Phase I “Human Capital Development” of the Royal Government, Angle 1, focuses on
strengthening the quality of education, sports, science, and technology to improve the socio-
economic situation and enhance the role of women as the backbone of national economic and social
development.
The Ministry of Education, Youth and Sport has continued to implement policies, plans, and
programs in the education sector to develop human capital, including strengthening and protecting
women's rights and promoting gender equality in the education sector. MoEYS has set out the
following directions.
− Ensure gender equality, equitable and inclusive access and learning quality across all
education services, both in informal and informal system.
− Ensure increased participation of female education staff in the provision of education
services
− Ensure a positive educational environment and positive social behaviour to respond to
gender
− Build schools closer to people's homes to encourage female students from poorer families to
attend, to reduce travel costs and ensure safety.
− Build a slope for students with disabilities.
− Building dormitories for students, pedagogical students, residency students, such as in
schools, pedagogical schools, general and technical high schools, and universities.
− Implement the Child Friendly School Program and the School Counselor Program.
− Promote awareness of the importance of educating women who invest from mother to child,
as well as paying attention to society's attitude towards daughters.
− Provide scholarships for general priority students, outstanding students, female priority, poor
priority, priority remote areas, and students with disabilities, especially female students.
− Develop textbooks, study materials, improve teaching and learning activities to be engaging,
especially promoting women and girls without discrimination on the basis of gender.
− Provide sexual and reproductive health education, prevention of HIV, AIDS, and other
communicable diseases.
1

2. Results Achieved in 2021-2025
Implementing the Gender Mainstreaming Strategic Plan in Education Sector 2021-2025, the
Ministry aims to “ensure that children and adults have access to inclusive, equitable access to
educational services and achieve quality and effective learning outcomes”, embedded with three
goals: 1) Ensure and achieve gender equality in access and quality teaching and learning across all
education services in and around the country; 2) ensure increased participation of female education
workers at all levels and in the provision of education services, and 3) ensure the building of an
educational environment andcultivate positive social behaviour to respond to gender.
Many of the key achievements and progress in implementing the Gender Mainstreaming Strategic
Plan in the past education sector have been measured as follows:
2.1 Cultivating positive social behaviours toward girl education and gender equality among
those involved in the education sector
Early Childhood Education
Children received all types of early childhood education services have increased from year to year.
In the 2024-2025 school year, children who have received all types of educational services in all types
are 425,539 increased 24,580 students; female students are 211,913 increased 10,623. The total
number of 3-year-olds is 56,487 increased 2,064; female 3-year-old students are 29,012 increased
711. Children aged 4-years old are 138,868 children increased 11,282; female 4-year-old children are
69,710 increased 4,958. Children aged 5-years old are 230,184 increased 11,234, female 5-year-old
children are 113,191 increased 5,307. Preschools of all types have 9,036 schools/places increased
145 schools/places located in 1,119 communes out of 1,652 communes nationwide.

Secondary Education
In the 2024-2025 school year, the total number of lower secondary school students was 712,298,
female students 371,188. Public lower secondary school students were 661,508, females 344 906;
private lower secondary school students were 50,790, females 26,282.
Total upper secondary students were 456,804, females 253,864. Public upper secondary students
are 413,352, females 231,497; private upper secondary school students were 43,452, females 22,367.
Public secondary education institutions have 1,825 schools, of which 1,244 is lower secondary
schools, and 581 is upper secondary schools. Private secondary schools have 442, in which of 114
are lower secondary, upper secondary schools have 328.
Of all 1,652 communes in the country, there are 107 communes (approximately 6.47%) that do not
have lower secondary schools. Among these communes, 46 communes in Phnom Penh do not meet
criteria for creating a lower secondary school in the commune. Communes in several other provinces
have faced similar issues in establishing lower secondary schools due to geographical and
demographic factors. Those provinces are Ratanakiri (with 20 communes do not have lower
secondary schools), Mondulkri (with 8 communes do not have lower secondary schools), Svay Rieng
(7 communes do not have lower secondary schools), Pursat (4 communes), Koh Kong (4 communes),
Battambang (4 communes), Preah Sihanouk (3 communes), Kampong Chhnang (3 communes),
Kampot (2 communes), Kandal (2 communes), Kampong Speu (2 communes), Preah Vihear (1
commune), and Kratie (1 commune). As for district level, out of 208 districts in the country, there is
one district, i.e. Okrieng Senchey district in Kratie province does not have an upper secondary school
yet due to lack of upper secondary school teachers and lack of proper physical school infrastructure
required for establishing a functioning upper secondary school.

Major Interventions for Gender Promotion in the Education Sector
These achievements are achieved, through the rollout of direct and indirect strategies and
interventions, with the aim of uplifting girls and women in key and inter-sectoral sub-sectors, as
follows:
− Construction of schools closer to people's homes to encourage students from poorer
families to attend, especially children, to reduce travel costs and safety;
16

− Construction of separate bathrooms and toilets for female students, male students and
students with disabilities, especially at the secondary level, to promote regular
attendance at school;
− Construction of slopes for students with disabilities.
− Construction of Dormitories for Pedagogical Students, Pedagogical Students, Pedagogical
Schools, General and Technical High Schools, and Universities;
− Implementation of Child Friendly School Programs and Establishment of School Council
Programs;
− Raising awareness of the importance of women's education, which invests from mother
to child, as well as paying attention to society's attitude towards daughters;
− Provision of scholarships for general priority students, outstanding students, female
priority, priority, poor, priority, isolated areas, and students with disabilities, especially
female students, to ensure that at least 60% of the number of scholarship recipients have
entered higher education;
− Providing and deploying adequate teachers, especially women's teachers, for teaching
students in the lower levels (preschool and primary)
− Developing curriculum, textbooks, learning materials, improving teaching and learning
activities to be engaging, especially promoting girls without gender discrimination;
− Preparation of technical education curricula on the subjects of accounting, tourism and
food processing to attract female students;
− Expansion of technical education and life skills education in secondary school in order to
provide the skills necessary for useful daily life, especially to female students, both while
studying and also in cases where they are unable to continue their studies to higher
grades;
− Incorporating gender mainstreaming into teacher training curriculums at all levels, and
building capacity for trainers, teachers and managers on gender knowledge, gender
mainstreaming into teaching and learning and education management;
− Providing training on gender-sensitive in education to trainers and principals/vice
principals of Teacher Training Colleges;
− Training on school-based management to principals of primary schools and secondary
schools by including gender mainstreaming and school development, gender and people
smuggling, gender and domestic violence, gender and health, gender and climate
change;
− Providing training on establishment, management and monitoring the girl counseling
programme to school principals/vice principals, and teachers at primary education and
secondary education;
− Providing sexual health and reproductively health education, prevention of HIV/AIDS
transmission, and other communicable diseases;
17

− Providing priority for girls and women in any competitions and selections.

3. Vision
Eliminate the gender gap to ensure that every citizen has access to full opportunities and equitable
and inclusive educational services.
4. Mission
To provide equal opportunities for children and adults with the same benefits, both enrollment,
academic continuity, and academic outcomes at all levels through:
Promoting gender equality and empowerment of women and girls in education sector;
–
Increasing the Role of Women in Leadership and Governance in the education sector;
–
Increase stakeholder attention to deliver gender-responsive interventions and improve
–
the quality of education in all education level both formal, non-formal and informal;
Promote research studies on gender disparities in the field of education to explore
–
possibilities and response measures.
5. Objectives
The Gender Mainstreaming Strategic Plan in education sector aims to “ensure that children
and adults receive equal, equitable, inclusive educational services and achieve quality and effective
learning outcomes”.
Ensure gender equality, equitable, and inclusive access to education and quality of learning
–
outcomes at all levels in formal, non-formal, and informal education
Ensure the increase of women’s participation in delivering education services
–
Ensure the establishment of a gender-sensitive educational environment and social
–
behavior.

9. Monitoring and Evaluation
Monitoring and evaluation (M&E) is a tool to set direction and to adjust the implementation of policy,
plan of action, and prioritized activities in the Gender Mainstreaming Strategic Plan in Education Sector
2025-2029.
To ensure effective, equitable, and successful execution, the Ministry is responsible for overseeing the
technical aspects of the plan, which relies on the proactive engagement and collaboration of all
departments, specialized units, and stakeholders. This includes the development and implementation
of targeted measures, supported by a robust and transparent monitoring and evaluation framework
aligned with the strategic objectives of the Gender Mainstreaming Strategic Plan in Education Sector.
Directorate General of Policy and Planning serves as the lead implementing agency, responsible for
overseeing all technical aspects of GMSP 2025-2029. It also coordinates the monitoring and evaluation
of strategies, activities, and priority programs under the GMSP, and will lead the development of the
next strategic plan for gender mainstreaming in education.
Gender Working Groups at both national and provincial levels are tasked with preparing biannual
reports on gender-related programs and activities. These reports must be submitted to the Gender
Commission of the Ministry at the end of each semester.
A Mid-Term Review of the GMSP 2025-2029 will enhance coherence, interconnectivity, and reliability
of this plan through a holistic assessment and evaluation of output, strategy implementation,
prioritized activities using performance-based management against workplan of action in the Gender
Mainstreaming Strategic Plan in Education Sector ensuring that every citizen has access to full,
equitable, and inclusive educational opportunities. The report will be used as a complementary
document to adjust the plan along the way and accelerate the implementation in the next two and a
half years.

description: The Ministry of Education, Youth and Sport has implemented the Gender Mainstreaming Strategic Plan in Education Sector 2025-2029 with significant progresses in terms of ensuring gender equality and positive educational environment, including the reduction of the gender gap in access to education services and access to quality learning, increasing the participation of female education personnel in the provision of education services and the development of social positive attitudes towards gender in education sector.
